titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to William Tyrrell Sr, 22 October 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
Summary of the present letter:
Willie is in the trenches. He describes an encounter with a German soldier at the front lines saying, "he had a rather serious reverse".

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Monday 22/10/17 My dearest Daddy I was dellghted to get your
long letter of 16th yesterday.
Weather here is very fine and
everything is very quiet. We
expect to go out of the trenches in
another two days. You say that the rebels are
saying we have had a reverse.
Well no one out here has heard
a word of it and I think we ought
to know. When I was up in the
front line this morning we spotted
a boche looking over the parapet
& I am afraid he had a rather
serious reverse. No news and I will write soon
again. I have some rather amusing
things to tell you but cannot
mention them for a few days
no news
Best love
Your most affec Son
Willie Of course I will give
something towards a wedding
present for the Pofe How
much should I give. Will
10/- or £1 do.
